metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated clear operational pain but has not quantified financial impact. On the 24 Feb discovery call, Jose Antonio Perez described approval cycles of 7-10 days even for low-value purchases ('if they don't move anything and let just the process flow, it would take a month or more'), users/finance/management all unhappy due to lack of visibility, and every sourcing event forcing manual Excel/email workflows regardless of whether a contract already exists ('we do not have a purchasing department itself, they were asking for every piece'). Perez stated the goal is 'transparency and visibility' and cycle-time reduction, with the pain owner being the decentralized buying process across maintenance, engineering, lab, logistics (10-15 buyers, 10 approvers, no central procurement). He also noted a new Mexican SAT tax mandate requiring formal contracts for every supplier, making contract management 'huge for us… otherwise it will be a nightmare.' However, no ROI, savings target, headcount reduction, or error-rate improvement was quantified. The lack of a quantified business case is a gap; Perez deferred pricing until volume inputs were provided (14 Jan call), but those inputs were never delivered, so no ROI model has been built. The partial status reflects confirmed qualitative pain with no financial quantification.

competition.narrative: Competition is SAP Ariba (ghost from prior evaluation) and the status quo (do nothing / stay manual). On the 14 Jan 2026 call, Perez stated that his boss (plant director) asked whether Zycus pricing would match the Ariba proposal from a prior evaluation, saying 'if it is the same as Ariba, it is a no go.' Karson positioned Zycus as differentiated on pricing transparency ('our pricing is also extremely transparent… user-based for most modules… very competitive, like, better or less expensive') and on the Merlin AI agent ('the agents that we have… are definitely our largest differentiators'). Perez confirmed AI is not a blocker for him ('for him, AI is not an issue; it's for the remaining of the company'). By the 24 Feb call, Ariba was treated as a settled loss (Devika: 'everything you've mentioned is solvable… can be done straight out of the box'), and Perez did not mention any other active vendor. The stronger current threat is the status quo: the 8-week hiring freeze with no restart date and no delivered success criteria or volume inputs signals inertia risk. If the hiring drags beyond mid-Jul or if other budget priorities emerged during the freeze, the pilot may be deprioritized. The wedge is Merlin + transparent pricing + full S2P fit vs Ariba's complexity and cost, but the wedge is untested because no demo or pricing has been delivered.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and owned. The operational pain is a 7-10 day approval cycle for every purchase ('even if we are buying a single pen'), driven by a 5-step sequential approval chain (head of area, controlling, finance chiefs, finance manager, commercial manager) running through a manual requisition process in the QAD ERP, with all upstream sourcing and procurement executed via Excel and email. Perez stated on the 24 Feb call that 'users are not happy, finance people are not happy, my boss is not happy' and that when he mapped the process for his boss, the boss said 'probably it's more important having the solution than we previously think about it.' The pain owner is Jose Antonio Perez (Quality auditor, acting project lead for the pilot facility), who reports to an unnamed plant director. Additional pain points include: (1) lack of visibility (only one person tracks sourcing status, everyone else is 'blindsided'); (2) unnecessary re-sourcing when contracts exist because no contract repository is checked; (3) manual 3-way match and CFDI invoice validation creating compliance risk ('I want that to be an RPA, I don't want it to be a person'); (4) new SAT contract mandate making the lack of contract management a legal/compliance issue. The pain is operational (cycle time, manual work, lack of visibility) and regulatory (SAT compliance), and Perez is the pain owner driving the pilot. The confirmed status reflects buyer-stated, role-owned pain with urgency.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is buyer-led but execution is frozen due to the IT lead's departure. The sequence Perez described on the 24 Feb call was: (1) map current processes and pain points (completed 24 Feb with the process chart walkthrough); (2) provide written success criteria and the English process documentation to Zycus (committed 24 Feb, never delivered); (3) Zycus delivers ballpark pricing for the pilot facility after receiving volume inputs (committed 14 Jan, inputs never provided); (4) finance manager reviews pricing against budget and provides feedback (blocked, no pricing delivered); (5) schedule onsite demonstration at San Juan Del Rio once IT lead and success criteria are ready (committed 7 May call, blocked by hiring freeze); (6) run pilot at the $18M Queretaro facility, measure ROI and user adoption, then scale to the 8 larger operations if successful (stated 14 Jan and 24 Feb calls). The approval chain within Rassini includes the finance manager (budget authority), Perez (project lead), and Perez's boss (referred to as 'my boss' on the 24 Feb call: 'I want my boss to have it in writing and say to me, that is what I want first'). However, Jesus Ochoa's departure on 7 May placed the entire process on hold ('we will put on hold this project until we find the successor of Jesus'). Eight weeks later (29 Jun), Next_Step shows 'no update' on hiring, meaning no restart date is set. The process is clear but stalled at step 2, with no path forward until the IT successor is hired and onboarded. The partial status reflects a defined process that is currently blocked.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are well-documented from the 24 Feb discovery session. Perez walked through the current manual process and named the evaluation priorities: (1) end-to-end visibility into sourcing and procurement status so users, finance, and management can track progress ('only one girl is sending an email with the status… otherwise everyone is blindsided'); (2) cycle-time reduction from 7-10 days to near-instant for contracted vendors ('it takes between 7 and 10 days… even if we are buying a single pen'); (3) eliminating unnecessary re-sourcing when a contract already exists ('no check for existing contracts leads to unnecessary sourcing cycles'); (4) contract management to comply with the relevant stakeholder tax authority mandates requiring formal contracts for every supplier ('contract management capability would be huge'); (5) automation of 3-way match and CFDI invoice validation to replace manual data entry and RPA workarounds ('we were thinking of an RPA running part of this 3-way match… I want that to be an RPA, I don't want it to be a person'); (6) modular, transparent user-based pricing that avoids Ariba-style 'sticker shock' (14 Jan call: 'if it is the same as Ariba, it is a no go'). Perez also mentioned phased rollout (source-to-contract first, then P2P) as a decision flexibility criterion. These criteria were stated by Perez and confirmed by Devika on the 24 Feb call ('everything you've mentioned is solvable… all of that can be done straight out of the box'). The confirmed status reflects buyer-stated criteria with solution alignment acknowledged.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers for when hiring completes: (1) compress the restart path by having demo-ready assets (Zycus process doc, scoping questions, pricing framework) prepared now so the new IT hire can move immediately; (2) multi-thread to the finance manager and plant director to de-risk single-point dependence on Perez and prove exec sponsorship; (3) build a quantified ROI model (7-10 day cycle cost, manual FTE burden, SAT compliance risk) to convert the qualitative pain into a financial case the finance manager can defend.

customer_preference.evidence: Perez explicitly included Merlin AI agent in pilot scope because 'it is part of your differentiators' (14 Jan) and confirmed 'for him, AI is not an issue' despite org-wide AI skepticism. Devika's solution confirmation ('everything you've mentioned is solvable… straight out of the box', 24 Feb) was met with agreement. However, no Zycus-favoring comparison vs Ariba beyond pricing transparency; no demo delivered; no active pull from buyer (8-week freeze, passive engagement).

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is a ghost from a prior evaluation that ended in sticker shock; Perez stated 14 Jan that if Zycus pricing matches Ariba 'it is a no go,' positioning Ariba as the price-sensitivity benchmark but not an active threat (no bake-off, no recent mention). The live threat is the status quo: 8 weeks frozen with no hiring resolution, no success criteria delivered, no volume inputs provided, and no exec sponsorship engaged. If the hiring drags past mid-Jul (10+ weeks) or if the finance manager's budget priorities shifted during the freeze, the pilot risks being deprioritized or quietly shelved. Zycus has clear product fit (full S2P + Merlin AI agent, transparent pricing, SAT contract compliance) but the wedge is untested because no demo or pricing has been delivered. The single strongest current threat is inertia / do nothing, driven by the hiring freeze and Perez's passive engagement.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong product fit against stated criteria but no formal validation yet. Zycus full S2P + Merlin scope matches the buyer's need for end-to-end visibility, cycle-time reduction, contract management (SAT compliance), and automation of 3-way match / CFDI validation. Devika confirmed 24 Feb that 'everything you've mentioned is solvable… all of that can be done straight out of the box.' However, no demo has been delivered, no success criteria document received, and no pricing presented, so fit remains unproven. The 8-week hiring freeze blocked formal validation.

hard.ais_why:
• Zycus has logged AI importance as 'Neutral', the explicit middle-of-the-road indicator. The agentic-platform discussion, Gen AI commentary and AI-RFP rating fields are all empty, so there is no captured buyer commentary on Merlin, agentic, GenAI or autonomous negotiation use cases anywhere in Salesforce.
• On the Feb 24 call (32 minutes) Jesus Ochoa kept the discussion on success criteria, ERP feature implementation timing and document translation. Jesus committed to send written success criteria, and the team agreed to draft a Zycus version of the prioritized pilot features. Every action item is classical S2P scoping language, with no AI references.
• The earlier Jan 14 and May 7 calls were similarly clean of AI signal. Combined with a 'Neutral' AI rating and explicit ERP-integration scoping conversations, the buyer is clearly anchored on traditional S2P process automation. There is nothing in the record to push this above the Resistant band.
